data.title: Municipal Bond Tax Exemption
data.description:
By Benjamin C. Pease

In this article, we consider the current environment and congressional discussions related to the potential removal or cap of the municipal tax exemption. We see the outright removal of the federal tax exemption from exempt municipal bonds as a low probability but not zero, and we believe that if the federal exemption were to be fully rescinded it would likely be done prospectively (not retroactively) — which would reduce some of the federal benefit. There may instead be a cap applied on municipal bond interest exemptions, possibly 28%, which would have a more likely path in our view to be applied retroactively but would still generate less savings at the federal level while creating financial pressure on state and local governments (SLGs). The cap is not a new consideration. It was proposed by the Obama administration in its 2013, 2014, and 2016 fiscal year budgets, and it will continue to face significant opposition, as it did then. We continue to monitor, as this is a highly uncertain environment. The municipal exemption is one of many areas being reviewed as a potential federal cost savings.

The Numbers

  • The full elimination of the federal exemption would generate approximately $250 billion in federal tax receipts over 10yrs — that is less than 1.5% of total federal tax expenditures.

  • The cost of a full elimination to state and local governments, reflecting the increase in borrowing costs (higher yields), would be around 35% to 40% higher, or an increase in costs to SLGs of $824 billion.

  • Alternatively, a 28% cap on the exemption might provide federal tax receipts of under $50 billion.

SLG Ramifications

  • Infrastructure projects (schools, affordable housing, etc.) would potentially be reduced or kicked down the road as their price tags increase.

  • Refinancing current debt burdens would become a much higher hurdle for SLGs.
  • With a cap or elimination of the exemption, the municipal bond buyer base might be reduced, limiting available capital to SLGs and making projects more expensive.

The Hurdles

  • The exemption has been in place since 1913, and the biggest impact to its removal would be the downstream effect to SLGs.

  • Historically, as a precedent, Congress has often applied changes to tax laws prospectively, not retroactively.
  • Common sense would suggest that removing the exemption retroactively would face intense scrutiny and legal challenges citing contract law and due process.
    • Currently issued bonds are generally deemed legal contracts with a promise to repay, covenants, official documents, and enforceability.
    • Contract Clause: Constitution Article I, Section 10
      • As this clause restricts only at the state level, we consider the argument may be deemed weak at pushing against a change at the federal level.
    • Due Process Clause: 5th Amendment and 14th Amendment
      • While providing legal challenge arguments, the federal government could justify the action based on federal legislative power over tax policy (Article I, Section 8).

  • The much lower potential tax receipts attainable from applying a 28% cap on the interest exemption may reduce the political will at the federal level when balanced against resulting state and local government impact, legal challenges, and investor impact on constituents.

Potential Market Impacts:

While we believe the probability of the exemption’s being fully eliminated or capped is low, we still consider the ramifications. A number of potential impacts should be considered:

  • A prospective (vs retroactive) full elimination of the municipal exemption

    • may favor existing muni bonds due to their future scarcity and supply versus demand, but there is a relative cap to how valuable an exempt municipal can effectively remain relative to Treasuries. When the tax benefit of the exemption is fully realized in the price, any further upside versus Treasuries is largely tapped out.
    • may result in lower-rated and/or higher-leveraged municipalities facing pressure as their ability to raise funds for operations may become strained and thus incur downgrade risk. These may underperform.
    • may result in longer-dated municipals that maintain the exemption having the highest demand and likely the biggest positive move in ratios relative to Treasuries.
    • may result in a lower probability of call exercises on exempt paper, as refinancing opportunity would decrease significantly, thereby increasing effective durations and (once again) demand for longer municipals that are grandfathered — potentially favoring lower coupons.
  • A retroactive (vs prospective) full elimination of the municipal exemption
    • would be a high-impact event for both SLGs and millions of individual investors (households). Again, we view a retroactive change as a low probability.
    • may undermine investor trust in the federal government and potentially lead to a risk-off.
    • may see existing municipals adjust market yields to an equivalency of taxable yields and a removal of a buyer base. The addition of more taxable municipals to the overall market may widen spreads from current levels.
    • may see credits experience downgrades resulting from higher financing costs.
  • A cap of the municipal bond income exemption for investors
    • may see some reduction in the buyer base for municipal bonds, putting pressure on liquidity and pricing
    • may see significant widening in municipal bonds immediately as the exemption benefit is reset into valuation and ratios to Treasuries.
    • may continue to see demand in the market for municipal bonds, but with adjusted values for both reduced liquidity and a reduced tax benefit.

The discussion about the municipal bond federal exemption falls into the overall landscape of 'uncertainties' in the market right now. This exemption has been considered and proposed in the past and will continue to face heavy opposition. While no one can say with conviction what lies ahead, we are considering the potential outcomes and weighing the conviction of our probabilities. We will continue to monitor.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The Cost Of Rising Tariffs
data.description:
By David W. Berson, Ph.D., CBE

President Trump presented his tariff plan on Wednesday afternoon at a Rose Garden Ceremony. Here is a summary of the plan:

  • All countries will face a minimum tariff on all imports to the US of 10 percent.
  • Countries with which the US has a goods trade deficit will face an additional “reciprocal” tariff. This tariff rate appears to be computed by taking half of the ratio of the dollar values of the goods trade deficit to exports. This method implies that the entirety of the goods trade deficit comes from tariff and/or non-tariff barriers that other countries have imposed on US exports.
  • Foreign-made light vehicles (autos, SUVs, light trucks, etc.) will be subject to a 25 percent tariff, with a tariff on parts to come shortly.
  • Mexico and Canada will continue to face 25 percent tariffs (based on the assumption that they have not done enough to curb fentanyl and illegal immigration).
  • Products that are part of the USMCA are exempt from the new tariffs.

These tariffs may change – moving higher if other countries retaliate in response to the tariffs, or moving lower through successful negotiations. As a result, there is still a lot of uncertainty with regard to what ultimate tariff rates will be. As of now, however, it is estimated that the weighted-average tariff rate has jumped to around 28 percent. At their peak, the infamous Smoot-Hawley tariffs that worsened and extended the Great Depression were around 20 percent.

Given the still uncertain path for tariff rates going forward, depending in part on how other countries respond, it is difficult to determine what the impact of the tariffs will be. But in general, tariffs are a tax on goods coming into the US from abroad. Firms can pass along the tariffs to consumers, reduce their profit margins, or (more likely) choose some combination of the two. As a result, import prices will likely increase, particularly in industries with smaller profit margins. In the near term, this means that measured inflation will increase. Whether or not those near-price changes will cause longer-term inflation depends upon whether the Federal Reserve eases monetary policy in response to likely slower growth and/or the near-term price hikes increase inflation expectations, changing wage/price-setting behavior. Economic activity is also likely to slow, as the tariffs act similarly to a large tax hike on consumers (and businesses). Enough to cause a recession? That is still unclear, but recession odds have certainly increased significantly as a result of the tariff actions. The combination of higher prices and slower growth is a negative supply shock, similar to the jump in oil prices following the Arab oil embargo in 1973. That oil-price shock caused a decade of stagflation. This time, the supply shock is self-induced.

There are certainly unfair trading practices from other countries that make the trade deficit larger than it otherwise would be (the US has some of those practices, too). And it is also the case that some workers in the US have lost their jobs because of imports (and, unfortunately, the US was not good at supporting those displaced from their jobs). Even so, the primary reason that the US has large and ongoing trade deficits is that there is a shortage of domestic saving relative to investment. This imbalance necessitates a capital inflow from abroad – a capital account surplus that mirrors the trade deficit. Without this, interest rates would have to rise in the US (probably significantly). But make no mistake, the most direct and least costly way to reduce the trade deficit is to increase domestic saving through reducing the federal budget deficit. And even with trade deficits and some workers losing their jobs as a result, the vast majority of households in the US are better off today because of trade.

Even if the tariffs work as the Trump Administration hopes they will, with new US factories, the resulting rise in manufacturing employment is likely to be modest. Industrial production for manufacturing industries in the US remains near record levels today, despite trade, but manufacturing employment has been trending lower since the late 1970s. It is mostly increases in productivity, not trade, that have resulted in less demand for manufacturing workers.

President Trump is correct that the US often faces trade barriers. But the costs of rising tariffs are likely to far exceed the benefits, especially if other countries retaliate by increasing their tariffs on US exports. Working to lower tariff and non-tariff barriers worldwide through trade negotiations and free-trade agreements is a far better way to achieve trade fairness, while allowing faster domestic growth.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ISM Services PMI: Lowest Reading Since June
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Institute for Supply Management (ISM) released its March Services Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI), with the headline composite index at 50.8 - below the forecast of 53.0. This marks the ninth consecutive month of expansion but the slowest growth since June 2024.

Here is an excerpt from the report summary:

Miller continues, “March saw drops in the readings of three (New Orders, Employment and Supplier Deliveries) of the four subindexes that directly factor into the Services PMI®. Only Business Activity saw an increase over February’s reading. Employment was the only one of these subindexes to drop into contraction territory after three straight months with all four in expansion. There has been a significant increase this month in the number of respondents reporting cost increases due to tariff activity. Despite an increase in comments on tariff impacts and continuing concerns over potential tariffs and declining governmental spending, there was a close balance in near-term sentiment, between panelists with good outlooks and those seeing or expecting declines.”

Here is a table showing the PMI's components.

Unlike its much older kin, the ISM manufacturing series, there is relatively little history for ISM's non-manufacturing data, especially for the headline composite index, which dates from 2008. The chart below shows the non-manufacturing composite.

The more interesting and useful sub-component is the non-manufacturing business activity index. The latest data point for March is 55.9, up from the previous month.

For a diffusion index, this can be an extremely volatile indicator, hence the addition of a six-month moving average to help us visualize the short-term trends.

Theoretically, this indicator should become more useful as the time frame of its coverage expands. Manufacturing may be a more sensitive barometer than non-manufacturing activity, but we are increasingly a services-oriented economy, which explains our intention to keep this series on the radar.

ISM PMI Comparison: Manufacturing vs. Services

The next chart offers a comparison between the two Purchasing Managers' Indexes provided by the Institute for Supply Management.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: VICI Properties: Elite REIT To Bet On
data.description:
VICI Properties (NYSE:VICI) is one of my largest REIT positions with several additions over the last couple of years. I first covered VICI in June 2024 and since then, the Company delivered a return of over 19% with some stock price turmoil at the beginning of 2025.

I still think that VICI doesn't get enough credit for its market position and unique value drivers derived from its industry focus - gaming properties. Yes, VICI has deviated slightly towards non-gaming experiential properties, but gaming remains at the core of not only its business orientation but also financial results (over 90% of ABR is generated through gaming properties).

VICI Is Elite, Because It's Unique

VICI doesn't invest in retail-oriented properties. It doesn't invest in the industrial space / housing / office space either. VICI concentrated on a specific type of properties associated with gaming - casinos. This sector is accompanied by unique value drivers, which are unparalleled within other property sectors.

VICI has strong negotiating power over its tenants, which results in favorable metrics and structure of contracts. You need some examples? One of the longest weighted average lease terms of ~41 years. Need some more? Over 40% of CPI-linked rent escalators in 2025 with a long-term goal to reach 90% by 2035, 100% rent collection in 2020, 100% occupancy, 90% rent-roll with parent guarantees, and above-average same-store rent growth.

VICI's assets are absolutely mission-critical. What's the cause of such a strong negotiating position? Necessity. VICI's properties lie at a very core of its tenants businesses - none can afford to simply move locations or default on rent payments. That's how VICI upheld 100% rent collection even during COVID-19 pandemic and has 100% occupancy rate. Since the properties are associated with a very strong brand factor, tenants' ability to move locations is highly limited, especially given regulation - gambling licenses are often tied to a property instead of entity. Do you believe that Caesars Entertainment (CZR) would have its business and financial results intact if it gave up on one of the pearls of Las Vegas - Caesars Palace? I don't. In this niche, brand matters.

On top of that, VICI generates ~48% of its gaming ABR in Las Vegas, the capital city of gambling where the Company has 10 trophy assets located on the classical LA strip.

Here's How VICI Drives Shareholder Returns

Once we learnt about the unique place VICI holds in the market, let's take a second to appreciate its value proposition. Starting with the most intuitive driver of shareholder returns - dividends, VICI managed to record one of the highest compound annual growth rate of distributions among triple net lease REITs. During the 2019–2024 period, VICI scored as high as 7% dividend per share CAGR, reaching $1.73 DPS in Q4 2024 (annualized).

On top of that, VICI ensures growth with an above-average same-store rent increases amounting to ~1.7% on average. It's important to highlight that VICI's focus on securing CPI-linked contractual rent escalators and over 40 years WALT play a major role in the long-term value creation. How so? Combine it with triple net lease structure of its agreements that involve the tenant covering the substantial part of costs associated with maintaining and operating the property. That leads this single-digit rent escalators to strongly impact the bottom line.

In terms of investment strategy, VICI engages in real estate acquisitions of a relatively large scale (38 bowling venues as an example), VICI property growth fund, and credit solutions within the experiential sector.

All these activities are aimed to drive the AFFO per share growth and create value for shareholders, which proves successful year after year. For instance, VICI increased its AFFO per share by over 5% in 2024:

For the full year 2024, AFFO per share was $2.26, an increase of 5.1%, compared to $2.15 for the full year 2023.

Our results highlight our highly efficient triple net model, given the increase in adjusted EBITDA as a proportion of the corresponding increase in revenue. Our margins continue to run strong in the high 90% range, when eliminating non-cash items.

Valuation Creates Another Return Catalyst - I Remain Bullish

Despite truly elite metrics, unique positioning, leadership position in the niche, and several paths to realize growth, VICI remains modestly valued with a forward-looking P/FFO multiple below 12x.

As a result, VICI presents a lot of potential with a double-digit total return opportunity. Naturally, the return potential is not as high as it was when I first covered VICI or when I covered it the last time. For this reason, I downgrade my rating from 'strong buy' to 'buy', but don't get me wrong - I will gladly increase my position in the case of any material pullback or once VICI's share in my portfolio decreases.

I view VICI as a package solution consisting of over 5% dividend yield, attractive valuation creating an opportunity for multiple expansion, and several internally-and-externally-driven growth prospects.

While the interest rate environment remains uncertain, VICI would surely benefit from interest rate cuts through a more attractive transactional market and improved prospects of debt refinancing given its meaningful maturities in the upcoming years.

Regardless, I consider its balance sheet manageable given the 6.4 years of weighted average maturity term, well-laddered maturities, and Net Leverage Ratio within the Company's target of 5-5.5x.

To conclude, I remain bullish on VICI but given the lower total return potential than in I expected in my previous coverages, I'm happy to assign VICI a 'buy' rating. Cheers!

data.title: Apple: 6 Reasons To Consider Buying Amid Tariff Uncertainty
data.description:
Shares of Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL) have come under heavy selling pressure in recent weeks amid continues worries about the negatives impacts on the company's business due to rising tariffs on China. As of this writing, the stock has now fallen nearly 19% so far in 2025 while the S&P 500 is down roughly 8% over the same time period.

While the stock has a long-history of delivering exceptional results for investors, it is currently out of favor and is rated a 'Hold' by Seeking Alpha analysts. There are four reasons why I disagree with this characterization and rate the stock a Buy:

1. Apple may receive some form of tariff exemption

2. There is a good chance that tariffs on China will be reduced

3. Apple's pricing power will allow it to mitigate the impact of tariffs

4. Improving regulatory environment

5. AI story remains intact

6. Valuation has become more attractive

1. Apple May Receive Some Form of Tariff Exemption

As of this writing, goods exported to the U.S. from China will face additional tariffs of 54% compared to the level as of the start of the year. Tariffs on goods produced in China are a challenge for Apple, as the country accounts for roughly 80% of Apple's production capacity. That said, I believe there is a good chance that Apple may receive some form of exemption. For starters, it is worth noting that the company received an exemption from tariffs imposed on China during Trump's first term. Apple CEO Tim Cook has developed a close relationship with President Trump and contributed $1 million to Trump's inauguration. Moreover, in late February 2025, Apple announced a pledge to invest $500 billion in the U.S. over the next four years, which aligns with Trump's global to boost U.S. production capabilities. Trump is clearly pleased with this announcement, and even mentioned it as part of his tariff announcement. While it remains uncertain whether Apple will receive any form of exemption, the fact that the company has announced a major U.S. investment plan and CEO Tim Cook has developed a close relationship with Trump lead me to believe the odds are good that Apple will receive at least some form of tariff relief.

2. There is a Good Chance That Tariffs on China Will Be Reduced

Another reason why the potential impact from tariffs might be less severe than market participants believe is there remains potential for Trump to lower the tariffs on China. Trump has repeatedly said that he is open to making a deal under the right circumstances and has said that if China were to agree to a TikTok sale, he would be willing to reduce tariffs. Furthermore, Trump may decide that the negative impact from his proposed Chinese tariffs are too much for U.S. consumers to stomach (in terms of rising prices) resulting in a deal to lower tariffs even if China is unwilling to offer significant concessions. Given the fact that China enjoys a large positive balance of trade with the U.S., I believe that it has more to lose from a trade war than the U.S. For this reason, I expect China to take a pragmatic approach to tariff negotiations and look to make a deal to blunt the potential impact.

3. Apple's Pricing Power Will Allow It to Mitigate the Impact of Tariffs

Some Wall Street analysts have estimated that tariff headwinds could result in a roughly 15% headwind to EPS. However, I view this estimate as too extreme, as I believe that Apple would be able to raise prices enough to offset much of the impact related to tariffs. As shown by the chart below, Apple is clearly a high margin business, with gross margins approaching 50% and net margins of nearly 25%. Moreover, Apple's biggest competitor in the smartphone business, Samsung is also likely to face significant impacts due to tariffs as it has significant production in India and Vietnam, which are set to face new tariffs of 27% and 46% respectively. Given this dynamic, I expect Apple and Samsung to both look to increase prices enough to offset any potential negative impact related to tariffs. Additionally, it should be noted that the average selling price of Apple products is fairly low relative to some larger ticket items which have been the target of new tariffs, such as automobiles. I believe consumers will be more willing to pay up a bit for their next smartphone (potentially a couple of hundred dollars vs the previous price) compared to larger ticket items such as cars, where the pricing impact due to tariffs is likely to be in the thousands.

4. Improving Regulatory Environment

While it has been a long time since I have published anything on Apple, one of my historical concerns with the company has been its exposure to regulatory challenges due to its dominant market position. Apple is currently subject to an FTC case which alleges the company monopolized the smartphone market. The company has also been the target of continued regulatory challenges in Europe. Given the close relationship between Apple CEO Tim Cook and President Trump, I expect the FTC case will likely result in limited, if any, action against Apple. Additionally, President Trump has argued that Europe is treating American big tech companies unfairly. As a result, I expect that Trump could push Europe to take a more conciliatory approach to regulation of large U.S. tech companies, including Apple. I view the improving regulatory environment for Apple as an important positive, as regulatory risk has historically represented a major risk for the company. I view this risk as diminished in the near-term, which should be supportive of the company's business outlook.

5. AI Story Remains Intact

Apple is well positioned to benefit from the AI megatrend in that it will be able to offer new features, such as a refreshed Siri, which can enhance the value proposition of new models of the iPhone and could lead to new revenue streams. As a result, Apple could experience an accelerated upgrade cycle over the next few years, leading to solid earnings growth. That said, the company has experienced some headwinds in its AI roll-out and recently delayed the launch of its refreshed Siri offering.

While Apple is well positioned to benefit from the AI theme, it is important to note that the company has avoided the capital intensive investments that have made AI a potentially riskier bet for other companies such as Amazon, Microsoft, and Meta Platforms. As shown by the chart below, Apple's CAPEX to revenue has been substantially lower than these companies, and thus I believe Apple may actually be better positioned than some peers to deliver a solid ROE on its AI investments given the lower capital intensity. Moreover, Apple has less downside risk in the event that the AI spending cycle slows.

6. Valuation Has Become More Attractive

As of this writing, Apple shares are currently trading at roughly 28x consensus FY 2025 EPS. This valuation multiple represents a significant discount to the 35.3x forward P/E ratio that the stock had traded at as recently as December 2024. Moreover, I also view the stock's multiple as reasonable relative to its own historical trading range, as the stock has traded at an average P/E ratio of 30x over the past five years.

In addition to trading at a reasonable valuation relative to its own historical norm, I also believe Apple is trading at a reasonable valuation relative to peers. For example, Microsoft currently trades at 29x FY 2025 EPS and is expected to grow FY 2025–2027 EPS at rates of 11.7%, 13.9%, and 17.3% respectively. Comparably, Apple is expected to grow FY 2025–2027 EPS at rates of 8.3%, 12%, and 10.9% respectively. While Apple's consensus EPS growth rates are lower, I believe they are more conservative in that expectations for Apple's AI upgrade cycle are fairly conservative given the company's recent AI setbacks and potential tariff risk. Moreover, Apple has not been making the massive AI capital investments that Microsoft has, and thus has less risk in the event that the AI spending cycle slows down.

Risks to Consider

I believe the biggest near-term risk to the Apple bull case right now is potential multiple contraction. While I believe the stock's valuation has become a lot more attractive than was the case a few months ago, it is not extremely cheap relative to peers or its own historical range and could suffer additional multiple contraction if sentiment remains negative for the broader market. Another important risk to consider is that Apple fails to deliver on its AI investments, such as a refreshed Siri. While I think the company has set expectations fairly low in the near-term, market participants could lose confidence in the company's ability to benefit from AI if it is not able to show significant progress by the end of 2025.

Conclusion

Despite delivering investors solid long-term results, Apple shares are currently out of favor due to the potential risk posed by tariffs. I believe these risks are overblown, and the stock represents an attractive investment opportunity at current levels.

I rate Apple shares a Buy and would consider upgrading the stock to a Strong Buy if the valuation were to become even more attractive. I believe investors should pay close attention to developments related to the company's AI initiatives, and potential good news on that front could serve as the catalyst for a sharp move higher in the stock. Moreover, any good news related to tariffs could also send shares higher, given the negative sentiment towards the stock right now.

data.title: Voya Target Solution Trust Series Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

These portfolios are only offered as an investment option within variable products and retirement programs.

Market review

U.S. stocks continued their upward trajectory during the fourth quarter following Donald Trump’s presidential victory. The consumer discretionary and communication services sectors led, while materials and healthcare lagged. Large cap stocks outperformed small caps, and growth stocks significantly beat value stocks.

The U.S. bond market struggled during the quarter on concerns about sticky inflation, increased deficit spending and the Fed’s more conservative rate-cut path, as the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield rose by more than 80 bp, ending the quarter at 4.58%. The Fed cut rates by 25 bp in November and December. However, the central bank now projects just two rate cuts in 2025, reflecting a more cautious stance in response to strong economic data.

The U.S. election, marked by Trump’s proposed tariffs, increased global uncertainty, lead to further U.S. dollar strength and broadly weighed on international equities. Europe struggled with political instability in France and Germany and rising energy costs ahead of winter. In yen terms, Japanese equities rose due to strong corporate activity including strategic mergers, share buybacks and improved return on equity. Meanwhile, China dealt with weak consumer activity, property sector challenges and concerns about regulatory changes and economic restructuring. These combined factors caused international equities to continue to underperform.

Outlook

Global challenges and opportunities confront the markets in 2025. However, the U.S. remains attractive driven by post-election optimism, investment, productivity gains from deregulation, innovation and energy independence. Despite some softening, the U.S. labor market remains resilient. Inflation is expected to stay manageable, allowing the Fed to continue its gradual easing.

Supported by this positive outlook and President Trump’s pro-business proposals, we continue to overweight U.S. large and mid-cap stocks. Large-cap valuations appear expensive, but they are supported by strong corporate profitability. The increasing weight of the technology sector with its capital-light business models suggest that higher margins may be the new norm, making expectations more reasonable. With the election concluded, corporate spending is expected to resume and drive positive earnings growth as well. Mid-caps offer enticing valuations and are poised to benefit from a broadening expansion and lower borrowing costs and provide more financial stability than small caps.

Outside the U.S., pockets of weakness are becoming more enactment of monetary stimulus and its commitment to future fiscal measures indicate that policymakers are prepared to take action to meet their economic targets, which could provide a tailwind for emerging market stocks. In December, U.S. large-cap equities were reduced, and short-term bonds were increased. This move came after the post-election run-up and the elevated valuations, as we deemed it prudent to lock in some gains and diversify away from the momentum trade. In terms of manager changes, the BlackRock MSCI EM Index CIT was added to implement the tactical increase in EM equities.

In fixed income, U.S. IG core bonds offer decent yields and portfolio stability. While conviction on rates near-term direction is limited, we believe core bonds will hedge against equity risk due to the transition from inflation to growth risk that has occurred. While credit stress and defaults are expected to remain controlled, high yield spreads are very tight, leading us to prefer equities for risk allocation. In addition, an expectation that the U.S. dollar rally will continue and a positive U.S. economic outlook justify a preference for U.S. assets and an underweight in foreign currency denominated bonds.

Positioning

At the beginning of the period, Trusts were tactically overweight to U.S. mid-cap stocks and underweight in U.S. large-cap stocks. Emerging market (EM) equities were increased to remove an underweight by selling U.S. large cap and small cap. China’s recent enactment of monetary stimulus and its commitment to future fiscal measures indicate that policymakers are prepared to take action to meet their economic targets, which could provide a tailwind for emerging market stocks. In December, U.S. large-cap equities were reduced, and short-term bonds were increased. This move came after the post-election run-up and the elevated valuations, as we deemed it prudent to lock in some gains and diversify away from the momentum trade. In terms of manager changes, the BlackRock MSCI EM Index CIT was added to implement the tactical increase in EM equities.

Overall, Trusts continue to maintain a balanced posture with a preference for U.S. large and mid-cap equities, and core IG fixed income.

Performance

The Voya Target Solution Trusts’ primary performance objective is to outperform its strategic allocation composite benchmark over the long-term through tactical asset allocation, i.e., deviating from the composite benchmark over the short and medium-term and active manager selection. The benchmark return is the weighted average return of indices that represent asset classes included in the strategic allocation benchmark. Index returns are gross-of-all fees. The Trusts are generally rebalanced monthly and the strategic asset allocations are updated annually to reflect changes to our capital market assumptions. In the fourth quarter of 2024, Trusts’ outperformed their strategic allocation benchmarks. Tactical asset allocation was a detractor, but manager selection contributed.

Tactical asset allocation had a negative impact on performance during the period. Trusts’ tactical underweights in U.S. large cap and overweights to EM equities was the main detractor, as large U.S. stocks delivered another period of positive performance, helped by continued strong corporate earnings and despite sharply rising long-term interest rates. EM equities struggled due to geopolitical tensions, economic uncertainties in key regions and a stronger U.S. dollar.

Underlying managers’ relative results were positive across the Trusts. Strategies that contributed most to excess returns in the quarter were Voya Mid Cap Growth, Voya Large Cap Value and Wellington International Opportunities. The biggest detractors in the quarter were Voya VACS Series MCV, MFS Growth Equity and Voya VACS Series EME.

You should consider the investment objectives, risks, and charges and expenses of the variable product and its underlying fund options; or mutual funds offered through a retirement plan, carefully before investing. The prospectuses / prospectus summaries / information booklets contain this and other information, which can be obtained by contacting your local representative or by calling (800) 992-0180. Please read the information carefully before investing.

Disclaimer

Past performance is no guarantee of future returns. All investing involves risks of fluctuating prices and the uncertainties of rates of return and yield inherent in investing. All security transactions involve substantial risk of loss. Please reference your client statement for a complete review of recent transactions and performance.

This commentary has been prepared by Voya Investment Management for informational purposes. Any opinions expressed herein reflect our judgment and are subject to change. Certain of the statements contained herein are statements of future expectations and other forward-looking statements that are based on management’s current views and assumptions and involve known and unknown risks and uncertainties that could cause actual results, performance or events to ditter materially from those expressed or implied in such statements. Actual results, performance or events may ditter materially from those in such statements due to, without limitation, (1) general economic conditions, (2) performance of financial markets, (3) interest rate levels, (4) increasing levels of loan defaults (5) changes in laws and regulations and (6) changes in the policies of governments and/or regulatory authorities.

The opinions, views and information expressed in this commentary regarding holdings are subject to change without notice. The information provided regarding holdings is not a recommendation to buy or sell any security. Fund holdings are fluid and are subject to daily change based on market conditions and other factors.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 04/04/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: VLU: There Are Better Options To Find S&P 1500 Exposure
data.description:
What is VLU?

The SPDR S&P 1500 Value Tilt ETF (NYSEARCA:VLU) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund (also known as ETF) that invests in American listed stocks representing the S&P 1500, a mix of large, mid, and small-cap stocks. The point of this ETF is to have a cheap, passively managed option to track outperforming stocks within the S&P 1500 index, which tracks almost the full U.S. index, with the fund manager rebalancing and reviewing the fund quarterly or less for investment opportunities.

VLU's benchmark target is the S&P 1500 Low Valuation Tilt Index, and this article will tell you whether it's worth investing with VLU or another asset manager/fund when thinking about investing in a U.S. market index. This Index adjusts the weighting approach of the S&P Composite 1500 Index, overweighting stocks with relatively low/cheap valuations and underweighting those with higher/richer valuations. It is to be noted that the S&P Composite 1500 Index, a relatively key benchmark for the U.S. equity market, is a market-cap-weighted blend of the large-cap S&P 500 Index, the S&P MidCap 400 Index, and the S&P Small Cap 600 Index.

Passive investing ETF's mean that the fund managers don't make many trades, and only rebalance every once in a while, versus active managers, who have the mandate to buy and sell stocks more often if they feel that there's a compelling investment opportunity. I've worked in capital markets credit risk in the fund business at one of the largest banks in Canada, and have found that more often than not, these actively managed ETFs are simply not worth the high fees.

After further review, my analysis shows that VLU is the best investment vs. others passively managed peers. The fund's benchmark has underperformed others passively managed and low fee S&P 1500 tracking ETF's over the long term with higher fees. I recommend investors buy the SPDR Portfolio S&P 1500 Composite Stock Market ETF (SPTM) if investors want passively managed, low fee options for this benchmark with higher returns.

Fund Breakdown

VLU was launched on October 24, 2012, by State Street Global advisors with a mandate to seek to provide investment results that, before fees and expenses, correspond generally to the total return performance of the S&P 1500 Low Valuation Tilt Index. The fund overweights stocks with relatively low valuations and underweights stocks with relatively high valuations, noting that the managers seek "stocks that exhibit the strongest value characteristics based on: price to book ratio, price to earnings ratio, price to cash flow ratio, price to sales ratio, and dividends paid". Unfortunately though, if you look at the returns on many of these funds that try to passively trim the fat, so to speak, there isn't always a link to see the risk vs. reward returns that are mentioned in the prospectus strategy as the target.

That aside, the fund has been trading for years, with a NAV of $183MM and a daily volume of about 10,000 shares traded. The idea of the fund isn't bad - essentially, the managers are looking for undervalued stocks within the index to drive outperformance and yield. But upon further review of the ETF's results, I'm skeptical that the fund has fully achieved what it set out to do. Below is a chart over the past 3 years and 10 years, showing returns slightly below other passively managed peers with lower fee structures.

The fund also employs a 0.12% yearly ETF expense ratio for fees to the asset manager (as of Dec. 31/24), which is cheap, but still 4x higher than SPTM (0.03%). Ultimately, the fund is decent, but I don't think it's the best option based on the previous results and history.

Previous Results and Fund Portfolio

When comparing VLU's portfolio to peers, there are subtle differences in the portfolio breakdown, but not many. As of Dec. 31, 2024, the portfolio mostly resembles the S&P 1500, with a lower weighting in technology, and slightly higher weights in financials and consumer staples. Overall though, this fund is marginally different from the benchmark and its similar, though a bit lagging, performance proves it.

From a risk perspective, a beta of 0.94 notes that the fund will have less volatility than the benchmark, which makes sense for a value fund. That has borne out in past results with lower lows and some, but never long-lasting, bounce backs. Additionally, when evaluating VLU's risk-adjusted returns, I analyzed the Sharpe ratio to see whether the fund has generated excess returns over the risk-free rate when adjusted for total volatility. Unfortunately, the Sharpe ratio for the fund is 0.28, well below the score of 1+ to prove strong risk-adjusted returns. And from a macroeconomic perspective, small-cap growth stocks are more sensitive to interest rate fluctuations. With the Federal Reserve taking a wait and see approach to rate cuts given upcoming tariffs, mid and small-cap stocks may be left in limbo, after the last few years of underperformance due to higher borrowing costs. Given VLU's underperformance vs. peers, I'd rather buy SPTM if I expect a rally in the total U.S. market to occur.

Risks and Bottom Line

The bottom line for this fund, in my opinion, is that the juice is not worth the squeeze. There's simply not enough good news over the past 10 years to justify this fund strategy, and investors can simply get normal S&P 1500 exposure through SPTM. VLU doesn't have enough differentiation and hasn't outperformed to convince me it's a better ETF alternative than other passive counterparts. It's also less liquid, with only ~$1.85MM of daily volume, compared to ~$43MM for SPTM. With Trump announcing tariffs and the market already looking shaky, I'm already weary of a full 100% equity portfolio, and if you're thinking about a total U.S. market index, I don't think VLU is the best option of the bunch.

data.title: ECH: Well Positioned For 2026 And Beyond
data.description:
Opportunity Overview

Chile's stock market has been through a lot of turbulence since 2019. Political tensions elevated in 2019, and Covid economic shocks briefly sent the economy into a recession in 2023.

However, the market has had a strong start to 2025 and has finally caught up to emerging markets after months of underperformance.

Even after bouncing back in the past 6 months, the iShares MSCI Chile ETF (BATS:ECH) trades at a slight discount to MSCI Emerging markets. It also trades at a strong discount to its historical average. The market may rerate Chile in 2026 once the election passes and there is more clarity about global economic conditions.

Chile looks positioned for a long term bull market after it goes through some of the challenges of 2025. Trade war tensions will be very strong this month, likely resulting in a substantial pullback in emerging market equities. Chile will also be holding its elections in November this year, which may add to this volatility.

Chile's economy has improved substantially, as seen by its Q4 performance. The market looks like an absolute bargain relative to other emerging market equities, and 2025 still looks like a solid entry point, especially if there are strong pullbacks this month.

The iShares MSCI Chile ETF is a solid vehicle for Chile's stock market, with strong exposure to banking and materials stocks. This ETF looks positioned to continue outperforming emerging market equities in 2025 and 2026. Any potential dips, caused by the trade war or domestic political concerns, would create an even better entry point. I think the pros of the long-term potential and relative value here outweigh the short-term risks Chile will experience this year.

Economic Outlook

Chile's economy looks like it is heading in a positive direction in 2025, which should help support equity market sentiment. Chile's economy has quickly bounced back from the 2023 recession, and there will be more political clarity after the country's elections later this year.

Recent Growth Picks Up: Chile's economy had much slower growth at the start of 2024, resulting in a pullback in the equity markets. However, the country's Q4 2024 GDP growth was 4%, approaching the highs of 2022.

Chile's GDP Growth

This is an encouraging sign, although growth will be a bit slower in 2025 and 2026. The World Bank is calling for 2.2% growth in Chile in 2025–2026. This growth will be slightly lower than that of peers like Argentina, Colombia, Peru, and Brazil.

Chile mainly exports to China and the United States, so trade war tensions will have a strong impact on its growth potential. On a positive note, Chile's main export is copper, and the price movement of copper will likely be very favorable this decade due to new sources of demand from EVs/clean tech and also because of supply concerns.

Additional rate cuts could also help support further growth. Chile's Central Bank has still been holding rates steady and may be several quarters away from rate cuts. Chile's Central bank hasn't cut rates since December 2024, when it cut rates by 25 bps.

At the moment, Chile's Central Bank is currently targeting a 3% inflation rate in 2025–2026 and will need to see several months of stable inflation before cutting rates again.

Inflation peaked at around 14% in 2022 and has since improved substantially, although it has risen slightly since the beginning of 2024.

Chile Inflation Rate

Mining Investments: The growth of Chile's mining industry should support economic growth, as the government plans to invest over $80 billion in new mining projects through 2033. Around 90% of new mining investments have targeted copper mining.

The demand for copper should grow in the lower single digits this year, which could result in a supply deficit in 2025. In the long run, the demand for copper will be strongly driven by traditional sources, as well as new sources like electric vehicles and clean technology.

This is one long-term catalyst that makes it worthwhile to ignore some of the short term challenges this year, especially since the market still appears priced for these political and trade war shocks.

Public Debt: Many emerging markets had to take on records amount of debt following Covid, which put a lot of strain on these economies. Public debt in emerging markets has risen by around 8 percentage points since 2019.

Chile's government has been no exception, but it is currently in a much better position because of the low base effect.

ETF and Equity Markets

The iShares Chile ETF only invests in 25 companies and has a slightly higher management fee of 0.59%. The yield is not that attractive, but there are better options available if you target ADRs in specific sectors like banking.

Valuation thoughts: One intriguing fact about this ETF is that it trades at a substantial discount to MSCI Emerging Markets. Historically, Chile's valuation has been much higher, and it appears that the market is beginning to turn the corner following its brief recession and political concerns during 2022–2023.

Chile's Historical P/E Ratio

Trends have been positive in 2025, and the market's PE ratio will likely be able to expand in 2026 after the trade war risks and the November election passes.

Companies: Its top 3 holdings, Banco Santander Chile (BSAC), Banco de Chile (BCH), and Sociedad Química y Minera de Chile (SQM) collectively account for around 35% of the ETF's assets. Banks began to break out during the second half of 2023, while Chile's main lithium mining company pulled back strongly in 2023 due to weak industry sentiment.

Chile's banks are still attractively valued and offer a high dividend yield, while the overall ETF offers a much lower yield. These banking companies can be a solid alternative for dividend investors who also want exposure to the economy.

Industry Info: Other notable standouts about this ETF include the fact that it is heavily invested in consumer names (circa 26% of assets) yet still has a very low valuation.

Some of the other cheaper regional markets like Colombia and Brazil trade at single digit PE ratios, but partially because of the industry concentration.

Colombia: Colombia's MSCI Index primarily consists of banking stocks and also invests in other industries like materials and utilities.

Brazil: Brazil, which trades at 11x earnings, also has a higher weighting in areas like banking and materials, and lower weighting in consumer themes.

This is not to write off Brazil and Colombia, which look like some of the most stellar value plays in emerging markets, but to note that Chile's stock market discount is significant both to regional peers, emerging markets, and its historical average.

Risks and Final Thoughts

Election in 2025: Chile will hold its elections in November 2025, and the markets will likely be very volatile before and after this event, perhaps similar to some of the 2019 selloffs. There have also been some protests in recent months.

One factor to note is that Chile's stock market has been very resilient amid multiple political setbacks, specifically since November 2019 when there were widespread protests in the country. Historically, Chile has been able to bounce back from these risks in the long run, but short-term pain is likely inevitable.

This ETF is only down 6% since these conflicts, including two rejections of a new proposed constitution in 2022 and 2023.

Trade War: The biggest threat approaching is the upcoming trade war, which sent US equities tumbling on Wednesday. Emerging markets, including Chile, could sell off in April, which will create a nice entry point for Chilean equities because they are already undervalued. Chile's top export destinations are the United States and China, so it will likely be very volatile in the upcoming months before the country's elections. While US markets tumbled on Wednesday, this ETF opened in the green. Chile is not a primary target of trade war tensions, but could be indirectly impacted later this year.

The biggest risk with this ETF at the moment is that there are many short-term threats that could result in pullbacks between April to November. If you are hesitant about emerging markets or want to keep more cash on the sidelines, this makes sense. However, Chile is widely underrepresented in many emerging market indexes and has a lot of hidden potential that many investors overlook. It absolutely makes sense to be overweight in Chile in this environment and to continue holding if you want emerging market exposure.

data.title: Top 50 High-Quality Dividend Growth Stocks For April 2025
data.description:
High-Quality Dividend Stock Investable Universe

On September 1, 2024, I started tracking an investable universe of what I believe to be 50 high-quality dividend growth stocks. You can find out more about the formation of this investable universe in the original article.

The objective of this investable universe is to present me with opportune investment ideas at any given point in time. As share prices swing up and down and business fundamentals change daily, my custom tracking tool updates the valuation ratings for each of these stocks and helps me figure out which ones I should focus my attention on.

The investable universe, as a whole, got off to a good start this year, but many of the individual components did not go unscathed in the recent market sell-off. The average loss for all 50 included stocks in March was 4.02%, compared to a loss of 5.86% for the SPDR S&P 500 Trust ETF (SPY) and a loss of 1.16% for Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). Here are the year-to-date returns:

Investable Universe: -0.92%

SPY: -4.56%

SCHD: +3.26%

Below is an update for this investable universe as of April 2, 2025.

Investable Universe

Here is a snapshot of the full investable universe including share price as of 4/2/25, current valuation rating, forward rate of return estimate, the valuation rating from 2/28/25 and whether or not it has changed.

My investment objective is simple, I want to identify "High Quality" businesses and I want to buy them at a fair or better price. I use this investable universe, along with the valuation model, to narrow my focus.

12 out of the 50 stocks in my universe had their valuation rating change since 2/28/25. 8 of those changes were favorable while the other 4 were downgrades.

In last month's update, we dove deeper into the 9 stocks that all had a favorable valuation rating and at least a 10% expected rate of return. This month we will look at the 8 stocks with the highest expected rate of return.

Monolithic Power Systems

MPWR tops out list with an expected rate of return of 23.93%. That return is made up of the following components:

  • 1.06% Forward Yield
  • 4.56% Return to Fair Value [FCF]
  • 18.31% Projected EPS Growth [3-5 years]

Valuation & Performance:

  • 22.98% 10-Year Free Cash Flow CAGR
  • 2.24% FCF Yield is 32.5% higher than the trailing average of 1.69%
  • Fair Price: $784
  • January: +7.72%
  • February: -4.14%
  • March: -4.82%
  • 2025: -1.72%

ResMed

RMD has the second-highest expected rate of return of 22.66%. That return is made up of the following components:

  • 0.95% Forward Yield
  • 7.71% Return to Fair Value [FCF]
  • 13.99% Projected EPS Growth [3-5 years]

Valuation & Performance:

  • 14.85% 10-Year Free Cash Flow CAGR
  • 4.24% FCF Yield is 81.9% higher than the trailing average of 2.33%
  • Fair Price: $405
  • January: +3.28%
  • February: -0.90%
  • March: -4.14%
  • 2025: -1.89%

MarketAxess Holdings

MKTX has the third-highest expected rate of return of 18.06%. That return is made up of the following components:

  • 1.44% Forward Yield
  • 6.03% Return to Fair Value [FCF]
  • 10.59% Projected EPS Growth [3-5 years]

Valuation & Performance:

  • 14.50% 10-Year Free Cash Flow CAGR
  • 4.04% FCF Yield is 51.3% higher than the trailing average of 2.67%
  • Fair Price: $319
  • January: -2.39%
  • February: -12.28%
  • March: +12.22%
  • 2025: -3.91%

Lam Research

LRCX has the fourth-highest expected rate of return of 17.96%. That return is made up of the following components:

  • 1.25% Forward Yield
  • 1.36% Return to Fair Value [FCF]
  • 15.35% Projected EPS Growth [3-5 years]

Valuation & Performance:

  • 25.03% 10-Year Free Cash Flow CAGR
  • 4.58% FCF Yield is 7% higher than the trailing average of 4.28%
  • Fair Price: $79
  • January: +12.21%
  • February: -5.32%

  • 2025: +0.94%

The Toro Company

TTC has the fifth-highest expected rate of return of 16.81%. That return is made up of the following components:

  • 2.06% Forward Yield

Valuation & Performance:

MSCI

MSCI has the sixth-highest expected rate of return of 16.02%. That return is made up of the following components:

Zoetis

ZTS has the eighth-highest expected rate of return of 14.55%. That return is made up of the following components:

  • 1.22% Forward Yield
  • 5.22% Return to Fair Value [FCF]
  • 8.10% Projected EPS Growth [3-5 years]

Valuation & Performance:

  • 19.39% 10-Year Free Cash Flow CAGR
  • 3.09% FCF Yield is 39.8% higher than the trailing average of 2.21%
  • Fair Price: $229
  • January: +5.21%
  • February: -2.14%
  • March: -1.55%
  • 2025: +1.36%

Final Thoughts

To reiterate, the objective of this investable universe is to help me identify which stocks may be opportune investments at a given point in time. While all 50 of these stocks are potentially high-quality businesses, it makes more sense to focus on the companies that have stronger track records, appear reasonably or attractively valued and present a desirable future rate of return.
